subject: Physics
question_text: The position of an object moving along the x-axis is given by x = a + bt 2 , where a = 8.5 m, b = 2.5 m and t is measured in seconds. What is its velocity at t = 0 s and t = 2.0 s? What is the average velocity between t = 2.0 s and t = 4.0 s?
question_html: The position of an object moving along the x-axis is given by x = a + bt2, where a = 8.5 m, b = 2.5 m and t is measured in seconds. What is its velocity at t = 0 s and t = 2.0 s? What is the average velocity between t = 2.0 s and t = 4.0 s?
solution_text: We know that, {tex}v = \frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} {tex}v = \frac { d } { d t } ( a + b t ^2){/tex} = 2bt = 5t m/s .....(i) At t = 0, from equation (i) we have v = 0, At t = 2 from equation (i) we have v = 10 m/s Average velocity = {tex}\frac { x ( 4 ) \ - \ x ( 2 ) } { 4 \ - \ 2 } = \frac { a \ + \ 16 b \ - \ a \ - \ 4 b } { 2 }{/tex} = {tex}6 \times b = 6 \times 2.5{/tex} = 15 m/s
solution_html:
We know that, {tex}v = \frac { d x } { d t }{/tex}
{tex}v = \frac { d } { d t } ( a + b t ^2){/tex} = 2bt = 5t m/s .....(i)
At t = 0, from equation (i) we have v = 0,
At t = 2 from equation (i) we have  v = 10 m/s
Average velocity = {tex}\frac { x ( 4 ) \ - \ x ( 2 ) } { 4 \ - \ 2 } = \frac { a \ + \ 16 b \ - \ a \ - \ 4 b } { 2 }{/tex} = {tex}6 \times b = 6 \times 2.5{/tex} = 15 m/s

subject: Physics
question_text: Obtain equation of motion for constant acceleration using the method of calculus.
question_html: Obtain equation of motion for constant acceleration using the method of calculus.
solution_text: From the definition of average acceleration, we have {tex}a = \frac { d v } { d t } \Rightarrow d v = a d t{/tex} Integrating on both sides and taking the limit for velocity u to v and for time 0 to t, we get {tex}\int _ { u } ^ { v } d v = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } a d t = a \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } d t = a [ t ] _ { 0 } ^ { t }{/tex} [a is constant] v - u = at v = u + at .....................(i) Now, from the definition of velocity, we have {tex}v = \frac { d x } { d t } \Rightarrow d x = v d t{/tex} Integrating on both sides and taking the limit for displacement x 0 to x and for time 0 to t, we get {tex}\int _ { x _ { 0 } } ^ { x } d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } v d t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } ( u + a t ) d t = v _ { 0 } [ t ] _ { 0 } ^ { t } + a \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { t }{/tex} x - x 0 = ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex} at 2 x = x 0 + ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex} at 2 ..........................(ii) Now, we can write {tex}a = \frac { d v } { d t } = \frac { d v } { d x } \frac { d x } { d t } = v \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} or vdv = adx Integrating on both sides and taking the limit for velocity u to v and for displacement x 0 to x, we get {tex}\int _ { u } ^ { v } v d v = \int _ { x _ { 0 } } ^ { x } a d x{/tex} ; {tex}\frac { v ^ { 2 } - u ^ { 2 } } { 2 } = a \left( x - x _ { 0 } \right){/tex} v 2 = u 2 + 2a(x - x 0 ) ........................(iii) Equations (i), (ii) and (iii) are the required equations of motion.
solution_html:
From the definition of average acceleration, we have
{tex}a = \frac { d v } { d t } \Rightarrow d v = a d t{/tex}
Integrating on both sides and taking the limit for velocity u to v and for time 0 to t, we get
{tex}\int _ { u } ^ { v } d v = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } a d t = a \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } d t = a [ t ] _ { 0 } ^ { t }{/tex}[a is constant]
v - u = at
v = u + at .....................(i)
Now, from the definition of velocity, we have

 {tex}v = \frac { d x } { d t } \Rightarrow d x = v d t{/tex}
Integrating on both sides and taking the limit for displacement x0 to x and for time 0 to t, we get
{tex}\int _ { x _ { 0 } } ^ { x } d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } v d t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } ( u + a t ) d t = v _ { 0 } [ t ] _ { 0 } ^ { t } + a \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { t }{/tex}
x - x0 = ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}at2
x = x0 + ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}at2 ..........................(ii)
Now, we can write
{tex}a = \frac { d v } { d t } = \frac { d v } { d x } \frac { d x } { d t } = v \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} or vdv = adx
Integrating on both sides and taking the limit for velocity u to v and for displacement x0 to x, we get
{tex}\int _ { u } ^ { v } v d v = \int _ { x _ { 0 } } ^ { x } a d x{/tex}; {tex}\frac { v ^ { 2 } - u ^ { 2 } } { 2 } = a \left( x - x _ { 0 } \right){/tex}
v2 = u2 + 2a(x - x0) ........................(iii)
Equations (i), (ii) and (iii) are the required equations of motion.

subject: Physics
question_text: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 20 m/s from the top of a multistorey building. The height of the point from where the ball is thrown is 25 m from the ground. How high will the ball rise? and how long will it be before the ball hits the ground? Take g = 10 m/s 2 .
question_html:
A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 20 m/s from the top of a multistorey building. The height of the point from where the ball is thrown is 25 m from the ground.

  1. How high will the ball rise? and
  2. how long will it be before the ball hits the ground? Take g = 10 m/s2.
solution_text: Let us first consider the vertical upward motion of ball upto highest point, we have initial velocity is, u = 20 m/s, final velocity is ,v = 0 and acceleration is, a = -10 m/s 2 , t = t 1 , x = ? Using the relation, v 2 = u 2 + 2ax, we have {tex}x = \frac { v ^ { 2 } - u ^ { 2 } } { 2 a } = \frac { 0 - ( 20 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times ( - 10 ) }{/tex} = 20 m. Hence, the ball will rise upto 20m and height from the ground = 20 + 25 = 45 m. From the given figure, we have upward motion A to B in time(t 1 ) and downward motion(B to C) in time(t 2 ) Also, Velocity at B is zero. v = u - gt From the top, the ball falls freely under acceleration due to gravity is given by ; {tex}\therefore \quad x = x _ { 0 } + u t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } g t _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Since x = 0, x 0 = 25 m, u =20 and g = 10 m/s 2 Therefore, 25 + 20 {tex}\times t- \frac12 \times10 \times t^2=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t^2-4t-5=0{/tex} on solving, we get t = 5 and t = -1 Time taken by the ball to hit the ground is given by = 5 s (reject t = -1 )
solution_html:
1. Let us first consider the vertical upward motion of ball upto highest point, we have
    initial velocity is, u = 20 m/s, final velocity is ,v = 0
    and acceleration is, a = -10 m/s2, t = t1,
    x = ?


    Using the relation, v2 = u2 + 2ax, we have
    {tex}x = \frac { v ^ { 2 } - u ^ { 2 } } { 2 a } = \frac { 0 - ( 20 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times ( - 10 ) }{/tex}= 20 m.
    Hence, the ball will rise upto 20m and height from the ground = 20 + 25 = 45 m.

  2. From the given figure, we have upward motion A to B in time(t1) and downward motion(B to C) in time(t2)
    Also, Velocity at B is zero.
    v = u - gt
    From the top, the ball falls freely under acceleration due to gravity is given by ;
    {tex}\therefore \quad x = x _ { 0 } + u t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } g t _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    Since x  = 0, x0 = 25 m, u =20 and g = 10 m/s2
    Therefore, 25 + 20 {tex}\times t- \frac12 \times10 \times t^2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t^2-4t-5=0{/tex}
    on solving, we get t = 5 and t = -1 
    Time taken by the ball to hit the ground  is given by = 5 s   (reject  t = -1 )

subject: Physics
question_text: Free-fall: Discuss the motion of an object under free fall. Neglect air resistance.
question_html: Free-fall: Discuss the motion of an object under free fall. Neglect air resistance.
solution_text: An object released near the surface of the Earth is accelerated downward under the influence of the force of gravity. The magnitude of acceleration due to gravity is represented by g. If air resistance is neglected, the object is said to be in free fall.
solution_html: An object released near the surface of the Earth is accelerated downward under the influence of the force of gravity. The magnitude of acceleration due to gravity is represented by g. If air resistance is neglected, the object is said to be in free fall.

subject: Physics
question_text: Galileo’s law of odd numbers: The distances traversed, during equal intervals of time, by a body falling from rest, stand to one another in the same ratio as the odd numbers beginning with unity [namely, 1: 3: 5: 7…...]. Prove it.
question_html: Galileo’s law of odd numbers: The distances traversed, during equal intervals of time, by a body falling from rest, stand to one another in the same ratio as the odd numbers beginning with unity [namely, 1: 3: 5: 7…...]. Prove it.
solution_text: Let us divide the time interval of motion of an object under free fall into many equal intervals τ and find out the distances traversed during successive intervals of time. Since initial velocity is zero, we have {tex}y=-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}{/tex} Using this equation, we can calculate the position of the object after different time intervals, 0 {tex}\tau {/tex} , 2 {tex}\tau {/tex} 3 {tex}\tau {/tex} . If we take (–1/2) g {tex}\tau {/tex} 2 as y 0 - the position coordinate after the first-time interval {tex}\tau {/tex} , then the third column gives the positions in the unit of y o . The fourth column gives the distances traversed in successive {tex}\tau {/tex} s. We find that the distances are in the simple ratio 1: 3: 5: 7: 9: 11… as shown in the last column. This law was established by Galileo Galilei (1564-1642) who was the first to make quantitative studies of free fall.
solution_html:
Let us divide the time interval of motion of an object under free fall into many equal intervals τ and find out the distances traversed during successive intervals of time. Since initial velocity is zero, we have {tex}y=-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}{/tex}
Using this equation, we can calculate the position of the object after different time intervals, 0 {tex}\tau {/tex}, 2{tex}\tau {/tex} 3{tex}\tau {/tex}. If we take (–1/2) g{tex}\tau {/tex}2 as y0 - the position coordinate after the first-time interval {tex}\tau {/tex}, then the third column gives the positions in the unit of yo. The fourth column gives the distances traversed in successive {tex}\tau {/tex}s. We find that the distances are in the simple ratio 1: 3: 5: 7: 9: 11… as shown in the last column. This law was established by Galileo Galilei (1564-1642) who was the first to make quantitative studies of free fall.

subject: Physics
question_text: Stopping distance of vehicles: When brakes are applied to a moving vehicle, the distance it travels before stopping is called stopping distance. It is an important factor for road safety and depends on the initial velocity (v o ) and the braking capacity, or deceleration, –a that is caused by the braking. Derive an expression for stopping distance of a vehicle in terms of v o and a.
question_html: Stopping distance of vehicles: When brakes are applied to a moving vehicle, the distance it travels before stopping is called stopping distance. It is an important factor for road safety and depends on the initial velocity (vo) and the braking capacity, or deceleration, –a that is caused by the braking. Derive an expression for stopping distance of a vehicle in terms of vo and a.
solution_text: Let the distance traveled by the vehicle before it stops be d s . Then, using the equation of motion v 2 = v o 2 + 2 ax, and noting that v = 0, we have the stopping distance {tex}d_{s}=\frac{-v_{0}^{2}}{2 a}{/tex} Thus, the stopping distance is proportional to the square of the initial velocity. Doubling the initial velocity increases the stopping distance by a factor of 4 (for the same deceleration). For the car of a particular make, the braking distance was found to be 10 m, 20 m, 34 m, and 50 m corresponding to velocities of 11, 15, 20, and 25 m/s which are nearly consistent with the above formula. Stopping distance is an important factor considered in setting speed limits, for example, in school zones.
solution_html:
Let the distance traveled by the vehicle before it stops be ds. Then, using the equation of motion v2 = vo2 + 2 ax, and noting that v = 0, we have the stopping distance {tex}d_{s}=\frac{-v_{0}^{2}}{2 a}{/tex}
Thus, the stopping distance is proportional to the square of the initial velocity. Doubling the initial velocity increases the stopping distance by a factor of 4 (for the same deceleration). For the car of a particular make, the braking distance was found to be 10 m, 20 m, 34 m, and 50 m corresponding to velocities of 11, 15, 20, and 25 m/s which are nearly consistent with the above formula. Stopping distance is an important factor considered in setting speed limits, for example, in school zones.

subject: Physics
question_text: Reaction time: When a situation demands our immediate action, it takes some time before we really respond. Reaction time is the time a person takes to observe, think and act. For example, if a person is driving and suddenly a boy appears on the road, then the time elapsed before he slams the brakes of the car is the reaction time. Reaction time depends on the complexity of the situation and on an individual. You can measure your reaction time by a simple experiment. Take a ruler and ask your friend to drop it vertically through the gap between your thumb and forefinger (Fig.). After you catch it, find the distance d travelled by the ruler. In a particular case, d was found to be 21.0 cm. Estimate reaction time.
question_html: Reaction time: When a situation demands our immediate action, it takes some time before we really respond. Reaction time is the time a person takes to observe, think and act. For example, if a person is driving and suddenly a boy appears on the road, then the time elapsed before he slams the brakes of the car is the reaction time. Reaction time depends on the complexity of the situation and on an individual. You can measure your reaction time by a simple experiment. Take a ruler and ask your friend to drop it vertically through the gap between your thumb and forefinger (Fig.). After you catch it, find the distance d travelled by the ruler. In a particular case, d was found to be 21.0 cm. Estimate reaction time.
solution_text: The ruler drops under free fall. Therefore, v o = 0, and a = – g = –9.8 m s –2 . The distance travelled d and the reaction time t r are related by {tex}d=-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}{/tex} Or, {tex}t_{r}=\sqrt{\frac{2 d}{g}}{/tex} Given d = 21.0 cm and g = 9.8 m s –2 the reaction time is {tex}t_{r}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.21}{9.8}} \mathrm{s} \equiv 0.2 \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution_html:
The ruler drops under free fall. Therefore, vo = 0, and a = – g = –9.8 m s–2. The distance travelled d and the reaction time tr are related by
{tex}d=-\frac{1}{2} g t^{2}{/tex}
Or, {tex}t_{r}=\sqrt{\frac{2 d}{g}}{/tex}
Given d = 21.0 cm and g = 9.8 m s–2 the reaction time is {tex}t_{r}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.21}{9.8}} \mathrm{s} \equiv 0.2 \mathrm{s}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: In which of the following examples of motion, can the body be considered approximately a point object?
question_html: In which of the following examples of motion, can the body be considered approximately a point object?

options.text: a railway carriage moving without jerks
options.explanation: Point object is used in kinematics branch of physics. It is an object whose dimensions are ignored or neglected while considering its motion. The size of a carriage is very small as compared to the distance between two stations. Thus, the carriage can be treated as a point sized object.

options.text: a spinning top moving on a curved path
options.html: a spinning top moving on a curved path

options.text: a spinning cricket ball that turns sharply on hitting the ground
options.html: a spinning cricket ball that turns sharply on hitting the ground

options.text: a tumbling beaker that has slipped off the edge of a table
options.html: a tumbling beaker that has slipped off the edge of a table

correct_option_text: a railway carriage moving without jerks

subject: Physics
question_text: The position-time (x-t) graphs for two children A and B returning from their school O to their homes P and Q respectively are shown in Fig. Choose the correct entries in the brackets below; ( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} ) lives closer to the school than ( {tex}\frac BA{/tex} ) ( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} ) starts from the school earlier than ( {tex}\frac BA{/tex} ) ( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} ) walks faster than ( {tex}\frac BA{/tex} ) A and B reach home at the (same/different) time ( {tex}\frac AB{/tex} ) overtakes ( {tex}\frac BA{/tex} ) on the road (once/twice).
question_html:
The position-time (x-t) graphs for two children A and B returning from their school O to their homes P and Q respectively are shown in Fig. Choose the correct entries in the brackets below;

  1. ({tex}\frac AB{/tex}) lives closer to the school than ({tex}\frac BA{/tex})
  2. ({tex}\frac AB{/tex}) starts from the school earlier than ({tex}\frac BA{/tex})
  3. ({tex}\frac AB{/tex}) walks faster than ({tex}\frac BA{/tex})
  4. A and B reach home at the (same/different) time
  5. ({tex}\frac AB{/tex}) overtakes ({tex}\frac BA{/tex}) on the road (once/twice).
solution_text: A lives closer to school than B. In the given x-t graph, it can be observed that distance OP < OQ. Hence, the distance of the school from the A's home is less than that from B's home. A starts from school earlier than B. In the given graph, it can be observed that for x = 0, t = 0 for A, whereas for x = 0, t has some finite value for B. Thus, A starts his journey from school earlier than B. B walks faster than A. In the given x-t graph, it can be observed that the slope of B is greater than that of A. Since the slope of the x-t graph gives the speed, a greater slope means that the speed of B is greater than the speed A. A and B reach home at the different time. It is clear from the given graph that both A and B reach their respective homes at the different time. B overtakes A once on the road. B moves later than A and his/her speed is greater than that of A. From the graph, it is clear that B overtakes A only once on the road.
solution_html:
1. A lives closer to school than B. In the given x-t graph, it can be observed that distance OP < OQ. Hence, the distance of the school from the A's home is less than that from B's home.
  2. A starts from school earlier than B. In the given graph, it can be observed that for x = 0, t = 0 for A, whereas for x = 0, t has some finite value for B. Thus, A starts his journey from school earlier than B.
  3. B walks faster than A. In the given x-t graph, it can be observed that the slope of B is greater than that of A. Since the slope of the x-t graph gives the speed, a greater slope means that the speed of B is greater than the speed A.
  4. A and B reach home at the different time. It is clear from the given graph that both A and B reach their respective homes at the different time.
  5. B overtakes A once on the road. B moves later than A and his/her speed is greater than that of A. From the graph, it is clear that B overtakes A only once on the road.

subject: Physics
question_text: A woman starts from her home at 9.00 am, walks with a speed of 5 km h -1 on a straight road up to her office 2.5 km away, stays at the office up to 5.00 pm, and returns home by an auto with a speed of 25 km h -1 . Choose suitable scales and plot the x-t graph of her motion.
question_html: A woman starts from her home at 9.00 am, walks with a speed of 5 km h-1 on a straight road up to her office 2.5 km away, stays at the office up to 5.00 pm, and returns home by an auto with a speed of 25 km h-1. Choose suitable scales and plot the x-t graph of her motion.
solution_text: Given: Speed of woman = 5 km/h Distance from home to office = 2.5 km Time {tex}= \frac { \text { Distance covered } } { \text { Speed of women } }{/tex} Time = {tex} \frac { 2.5 } { 5 } {/tex} = 0.5 h = 30 min When she returned, she covers the same distance in the evening by an auto. Given Speed of auto = 25 km/h Time {tex}= \frac { \text { Distance Covered } } { \text { Speed of auto } }{/tex} Time = {tex} \frac { 2.5 } { 25 } = {/tex} 0.1 h = 6 min The woman is at rest from 9:30 am to 5:00 pm and during this time, speed is zero The x-t graph of the motion of women from home to office and office to home is shown below.
solution_html:
Given: Speed of woman = 5 km/h
Distance from home to office = 2.5 km
Time  {tex}= \frac { \text { Distance covered } } { \text { Speed of women } }{/tex}
Time = {tex} \frac { 2.5 } { 5 } {/tex} = 0.5 h = 30 min
When she returned, she covers the same distance in the evening by an auto.
Given Speed of auto = 25 km/h
Time  {tex}= \frac { \text { Distance Covered } } { \text { Speed of auto } }{/tex}
Time = {tex} \frac { 2.5 } { 25 } = {/tex} 0.1 h = 6 min 
The woman is at rest from 9:30 am to 5:00 pm and during this time, speed is zero
The x-t graph of the motion of women from home to office and office to home is shown below.

subject: Physics
question_text: A drunkard walking in a narrow lane takes 5 steps forward and 3 steps backward, followed again by 5 steps forward and 3 steps backward, and so on. Each step is 1 m long and requires 1 s. Plot the x-t graph of his motion. Determine graphically and otherwise how long the drunkard takes to fall in a pit 13 m away from the start.
question_html: A drunkard walking in a narrow lane takes 5 steps forward and 3 steps backward, followed again by 5 steps forward and 3 steps backward, and so on. Each step is 1 m long and requires 1 s. Plot the x-t graph of his motion. Determine graphically and otherwise how long the drunkard takes to fall in a pit 13 m away from the start.
solution_text: Distance covered with 1 step = 1 m Time taken = 1 s Net displacement (forward - backward) = 2 steps = 2 m in every 8 sec. (5 sec forward and 3 sec backward) So he goes first 8 m in 8 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{8}{2}{/tex} = 32 sec and remaining 5 m (13 - 8) in next five steps which require 5 sec. So total time is 37 sec. The x-t graph of the drunkard's motion can be shown as:
solution_html:
Distance covered with 1 step = 1 m
Time taken = 1 s
Net displacement (forward - backward) = 2 steps = 2 m in every 8 sec. (5 sec forward and 3 sec backward)
So he goes first 8 m in 8 {tex}\times{/tex}  {tex}\frac{8}{2}{/tex} = 32 sec and remaining 5 m (13 - 8) in next five steps which require 5 sec.
So total time is 37 sec.
 The x-t graph of the drunkard's motion can be shown as:

subject: Physics
question_text: A car moving along a straight highway with a speed of 126 km/hr is brought to stop within a distance of 200 m. What is the retardation of the car (assumed uniform) and how long does it take for the car to stop?
question_html: A car moving along a straight highway with a speed of 126 km/hr is brought to stop within a distance of 200 m. What is the retardation of the car (assumed uniform) and how long does it take for the car to stop?
solution_text: Initial velocity, u = 126 km/hr = 35 m/s Final velocity, v = 0 s = 200 m Distance covered by the car before coming to rest, s = 200 m From third equation of motion, {tex}a{/tex} can be calculated as: v 2 - u 2 = 2as {tex}a = \frac { v ^ { 2 } - u ^ { 2 } } { 2 s }{/tex} {tex}a = \frac { ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } \ - \ ( 35 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \ \times \ 200 }{/tex} a = - 3.06 m/s 2 (Retardation) Now use the equation, V = u + at, we have {tex}t = \frac { V - u } { a } = \frac { 0 - 35 } { - 3.06 }{/tex} t = 11.44s
solution_html:
Initial velocity, u = 126 km/hr = 35 m/s
Final velocity, v = 0 s = 200 m
Distance covered by the car before coming to rest, s = 200 m
From third equation of motion, {tex}a{/tex} can be calculated as:
v2 - u2 = 2as
{tex}a = \frac { v ^ { 2 } - u ^ { 2 } } { 2 s }{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac { ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } \ - \ ( 35 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \ \times \ 200 }{/tex}
a = - 3.06 m/s2 (Retardation)
Now use the equation, V = u + at, we have
{tex}t = \frac { V - u } { a } = \frac { 0 - 35 } { - 3.06 }{/tex}
t = 11.44s

subject: Physics
question_text: A player throws a ball upwards with an initial speed of 29.4 ms -1 . What is the direction of acceleration during the upward motion of the ball? What are the velocity and acceleration of the ball at the highest point of its motion? Choose the x = 0 m and t = 0 s to be the location and time of the ball at its highest point, vertically downward direction to be the positive direction of x-axis, and give the signs of position, velocity and acceleration of the ball during its upward, and downward motion. To what height does the ball rise and after how long does the ball return to the player’s hands? (Take g = 9.8 ms -2 and neglect air resistance).
question_html:
A player throws a ball upwards with an initial speed of 29.4 ms-1.

  1. What is the direction of acceleration during the upward motion of the ball?
  2. What are the velocity and acceleration of the ball at the highest point of its motion?
  3. Choose the x = 0 m and t = 0 s to be the location and time of the ball at its highest point, vertically downward direction to be the positive direction of x-axis, and give the signs of position, velocity and acceleration of the ball during its upward, and downward motion.
  4. To what height does the ball rise and after how long does the ball return to the player’s hands? (Take g = 9.8 ms-2 and neglect air resistance).
solution_text: Irrespective of the direction of motion of the ball, acceleration (which is actually acceleration due to gravity) always act in the downward direction towards the centre of the earth. At the highest point, the velocity of the ball becomes zero and acceleration is equal to the acceleration due to gravity = 9.8 ms -2 in vertically downward direction. When the highest point is chosen as the location for x = 0 and t = 0 and vertically downward direction to be the positive direction of x-axis and upward direction as negative direction of x-axis. During upward motion, sign of position is negative, sign of velocity is negative and sign of acceieration is positive. During downward motion, sign of position is positive, sign of velocity is positive and sign of acceleration is also positive. Taking vertical upward motion of the ball, we have u = - 29.4 ms -1 , a = 9.8 ms -2 , v = 0, s = S, t = ? (where t be the time taken by the ball to reach the highest point where height from ground be s) As, v 2 - u 2 = 2as or v 2 - u 2 = 2gs {tex}\therefore 0 - ( - 29.4 ) ^ { 2 } = 2 \times 9.8 \times S{/tex} or S = {tex}\frac { - ( 29.4 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times 9.8 }{/tex} = - 44.1 m where negative sign shows that the distance is covered in upward direction. Also we know that, v = u + at {tex}\therefore 0 = - 29.4 + 9.8 \times t{/tex} or {tex}t = \frac { 29.4 } { 9.8 } = 3 \mathrm { s }{/tex} It means time of ascent = 3 seconds Since the time of ascent is always equal to the time of descent when an object moves under the effect of gravity alone. Hence, total time after which the ball returns to the player’s hand = 3 + 3 = 6 seconds
solution_html:
1. Irrespective of the direction of motion of the ball, acceleration (which is actually acceleration due to gravity) always act in the downward direction towards the centre of the earth.
  2. At the highest point, the velocity of the ball becomes zero and acceleration is equal to the acceleration due to gravity = 9.8 ms-2 in vertically downward direction.
  3. When the highest point is chosen as the location for x = 0 and t = 0 and vertically downward direction to be the positive direction of x-axis and upward direction as negative direction of x-axis.
    During upward motion, sign of position is negative, sign of velocity is negative and sign of acceieration is positive. During downward motion, sign of position is positive, sign of velocity is positive and sign of acceleration is also positive.
  4. Taking vertical upward motion of the ball, we have
    u = - 29.4 ms-1, a = 9.8 ms-2, v = 0, s = S, t = ? (where t be the time taken by the ball to reach the highest point where height from ground be s)
    As, v2 - u2 = 2as or v2 - u2 = 2gs
    {tex}\therefore 0 - ( - 29.4 ) ^ { 2 } = 2 \times 9.8 \times S{/tex}
    or S = {tex}\frac { - ( 29.4 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times 9.8 }{/tex}= - 44.1 m
    where negative sign shows that the distance is covered in upward direction.
    Also we know that, v = u + at
    {tex}\therefore 0 = - 29.4 + 9.8 \times t{/tex}  or  {tex}t = \frac { 29.4 } { 9.8 } = 3 \mathrm { s }{/tex}
    It means time of ascent = 3 seconds
    Since the time of ascent is always equal to the time of descent when an object moves under the effect of gravity alone.
    Hence, total time after which the ball returns to the player’s hand = 3 + 3 = 6 seconds

question_text: A particle in one-dimensional motion with zero speed at an instant may have non-zero acceleration at that instant.
question_html: A particle in one-dimensional motion with zero speed at an instant may have non-zero acceleration at that instant.

subject: Physics
question_text: A particle in one-dimensional motion with zero speed may have non-zero velocity.
question_html: A particle in one-dimensional motion with zero speed may have non-zero velocity.

subject: Physics
question_text: A particle in one-dimensional motion with constant speed must have zero acceleration.
question_html: A particle in one-dimensional motion with constant speed must have zero acceleration.

subject: Physics
question_text: A particle in one-dimensional motion with a positive value of acceleration must be speeding up.
question_html: A particle in one-dimensional motion with a positive value of acceleration must be speeding up.

options.explanation: False, Because if acceleration is positive, then it need not be necessary that particle will always speed up, it may slow down. The particle will speed up if velocity is also positive.

subject: Physics
question_text: A ball is dropped from a height of 90 m on a floor. At each collision with the floor, the ball loses one-tenth of its speed. Plot the speed-time graph of its motion between t = 0 to 12 s.
question_html: A ball is dropped from a height of 90 m on a floor. At each collision with the floor, the ball loses one-tenth of its speed. Plot the speed-time graph of its motion between t = 0 to 12 s.
solution_text: Taking vertical downward motion of ball from a height 90m, we have u = 0, a = 10m/s 2 , S = 90m, t = ?, v = ? {tex}\left.t=\frac{\sqrt{2 S}}{a}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 90}}{910}\right){/tex} = 3sqr 2 s = 4.24s {tex}v=\sqrt{2} a S=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times x 90}=30 \sqrt{2 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex} Rebound velocity of ball, {tex}u^{\prime}=\frac{9}{10} v=\frac{9}{10} \times 30 \sqrt{2}=27 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} Time to each the hight the highest point is, {tex}t^{\prime}=u-\frac{t'}{a}=27 \frac{\sqrt{20}}{10}=2.7 \sqrt{2}=3.81 s{/tex} Total time = t + t' = 4.24 + 3.81 +8.05s. The ball will take a further 3.81s to fall back to the floor, where its velocity before striking the floor {tex}=27 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} The velocity of the ball after striking the floor {tex}=\frac{9}{10} \times 27 \sqrt{2}=24.3 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} Total time elapsed before the upward motion of the ball + 8.05 + 3.81 + 11.86s Thus the speed-time graph of this motion will be as shown in the given below figure.
solution_html:
Taking vertical downward motion of ball from a height 90m, we have
u = 0, a = 10m/s2, S = 90m, t = ?, v = ?
{tex}\left.t=\frac{\sqrt{2 S}}{a}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 90}}{910}\right){/tex} = 3sqr2s = 4.24s
{tex}v=\sqrt{2} a S=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times x 90}=30 \sqrt{2 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex}
Rebound velocity of ball, {tex}u^{\prime}=\frac{9}{10} v=\frac{9}{10} \times 30 \sqrt{2}=27 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
Time to each the hight the highest point is, {tex}t^{\prime}=u-\frac{t'}{a}=27 \frac{\sqrt{20}}{10}=2.7 \sqrt{2}=3.81 s{/tex}
Total time = t + t' = 4.24 + 3.81 +8.05s.
The ball will take a further 3.81s to fall back to the floor, where its velocity before striking the floor {tex}=27 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
The velocity of the ball after striking the floor
{tex}=\frac{9}{10} \times 27 \sqrt{2}=24.3 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
Total time elapsed before the upward motion of the ball
+ 8.05 + 3.81 + 11.86s
Thus the speed-time graph of this motion will be as shown in the given below figure.

subject: Physics
question_text: Explain clearly, with examples, the distinction between: magnitude of displacement (sometimes called distance) over an interval of time, and the total length of path covered by a particle over the same interval; magnitude of average velocity over an interval of time, and the average speed over the same interval [Average speed of a particle over an interval of time is defined as the total path length divided by the time interval]. Show in both (a) and (b) that the second quantity is either greater than or equal to the first. When is the equality sign true? [For simplicity, consider one-dimensional motion only].
question_html:
Explain clearly, with examples, the distinction between:

  1. magnitude of displacement (sometimes called distance) over an interval of time, and the total length of path covered by a particle over the same interval;
  2. magnitude of average velocity over an interval of time, and the average speed over the same interval [Average speed of a particle over an interval of time is defined as the total path length divided by the time interval]. Show in both (a) and (b) that the second quantity is either greater than or equal to the first. When is the equality sign true? [For simplicity, consider one-dimensional motion only].
solution_text: The magnitude of displacement is the shortest between the initial and final positions of the body. The total path length of a body is the actual path length covered by the body in a given interval of time is called distance. For example, suppose a body moves from point A to point B and then, comes back to a point C taking a total time t, as shown below. Then, the magnitude of displacement of the body is given by = AC. Whereas, total path length = AB + BC When body move from A to B both quantities are equal to each other. Magnitude of average velocity is given by {tex}= \frac { \text { Magnitude of displacement } } { \text { Time interval } }{/tex} For the given body, Average velocity is given by {tex}= \frac { A C } { t }{/tex} Average speed is given by {tex}= \frac { \text { Total path length } } { \text { Time interval } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { A B + B C } { t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB + BC) > AC Suppose a person goes to a temple 1 km away from his house and comes back taking 1 hour to perform the journey both ways. Average speed of the man is given by = 1+1/1 = 2 km/h. But average velocity is zero because his displacement during the time of 1 hour is zero. So, in either case (a) and (b) the second quantity is either greater than or equal to the first quantity. Equality sign is correct when displacement is equal to the distance. It holds when body moves along a straight path.
solution_html:
1. The magnitude of displacement is the shortest between the initial and final positions of the body. 
    The total path length of a body is the actual path length covered by the body in a given interval of time is called distance.

    For example, suppose a body moves from point A to point B and then, comes back to a point C taking a total time t, as shown below. Then, the magnitude of displacement of the body is given by = AC.
    Whereas, total path length = AB + BC
    When body move from A to B both quantities are equal to each other.
  2. Magnitude of average velocity is given by  {tex}= \frac { \text { Magnitude of displacement } } { \text { Time interval } }{/tex}
    For the given body,
    Average velocity is given by   {tex}= \frac { A C } { t }{/tex}
    Average speed  is given by {tex}= \frac { \text { Total path length } } { \text { Time interval } }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { A B + B C } { t }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB + BC) > AC
    Suppose a person goes to a temple 1 km away from his house and comes back taking 1 hour to perform the journey both ways. Average speed of the man is given by  = 1+1/1 = 2 km/h. But average velocity is zero because his displacement during the time of 1 hour is zero.
    So, in either case (a) and (b) the second quantity is either greater than or equal to the first quantity. Equality sign is correct when displacement is equal to the distance. It holds when body moves along a straight path.

subject: Physics
question_text: A man walks on a straight road from his home to a market 2.5 km away with a speed of 5 kmh -1 . Finding the market closed, he instantly turns and walks back home with a speed of 7.5 km/h. What is the magnitude of average velocity, and the average speed of the man over the interval of time (i) 0 to 30 min, (ii) 0 to 50 min, (iii) 0 to 40 min? [Note: You will appreciate from this exercise why it is better to define average speed as total path length divided by time, and not as magnitude of average velocity. You would not like to tell the tired man on his return home that his average speed was zero !]
question_html:
A man walks on a straight road from his home to a market 2.5 km away with a speed of 5 kmh-1. Finding the market closed, he instantly turns and walks back home with a speed of 7.5 km/h. What is the

  1. magnitude of average velocity, and
  2. the average speed of the man over the interval of time (i) 0 to 30 min, (ii) 0 to 50 min, (iii) 0 to 40 min? 

[Note: You will appreciate from this exercise why it is better to define average speed as total path length divided by time, and not as magnitude of average velocity. You would not like to tell the tired man on his return home that his average speed was zero !]
solution_text: Time taken by the man to reach the market from home, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{1}=\frac{2.5}{5}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{h}=30 \mathrm{min}{/tex} Time taken by the man to reach home from the market, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2}=\frac{2.5}{7.5}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{h}=20 \mathrm{min}{/tex} Total time taken in the whole journey = 30 + 20 = 50 min 0 to 30 min Average velocity {tex}= \frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{2.5}{\frac{1}{2}}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} Average speed {tex}= \frac{\text { Distance }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{2.5}{\frac{1}{2}}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} 0 to 50 min Time = 50 min {tex}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{h}{/tex} Net displacement = 0 Total distance = 2.5 + 2.5 = 5 km Average Velocity {tex}= \frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}=0{/tex} Average Speed {tex}= \frac{\text { Distan ce }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{5}{\left(\frac{5}{6}\right)}=6 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h} \quad {/tex} 0 to 40 min Speed of the man = 7.5 mk/h Distance travelled in first 30 min = 2.5 km Distance travelled by the man (from market to home) in the next 10 minutes {tex}= 7.5 \times \frac{10}{60}=1.25 \mathrm{km}{/tex} Net displacement = 2.5 - 1.25 = 1.25 km Total distance travelled = 2.5 + 1.25 = 3.75 km Average velocity {tex}= \frac{1.25}{\left(\frac{40}{60}\right)}=\frac{1.25 \times 3}{2}=1.875 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} Average speed {tex}= \frac{3.75}{\left(\frac{40}{60}\right)}=5.625 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex}
solution_html:
Time taken by the man to reach the market from home, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{1}=\frac{2.5}{5}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{h}=30 \mathrm{min}{/tex}
Time taken by the man to reach home from the market, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2}=\frac{2.5}{7.5}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{h}=20 \mathrm{min}{/tex}
Total time taken in the whole journey = 30 + 20 = 50 min

  1. 0 to 30 min
    Average velocity {tex}= \frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{2.5}{\frac{1}{2}}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} 
    Average speed {tex}= \frac{\text { Distance }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{2.5}{\frac{1}{2}}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} 
  2. 0 to 50 min
    Time = 50 min {tex}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{h}{/tex}
    Net displacement = 0
    Total distance = 2.5 + 2.5 = 5 km
    Average Velocity {tex}= \frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}=0{/tex} 
    Average Speed {tex}= \frac{\text { Distan ce }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{5}{\left(\frac{5}{6}\right)}=6 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h} \quad {/tex} 
  3. 0 to 40 min
    Speed of the man = 7.5 mk/h
    Distance travelled in first 30 min = 2.5 km
    Distance travelled by the man (from market to home) in the next 10 minutes
    {tex}= 7.5 \times \frac{10}{60}=1.25 \mathrm{km}{/tex}
    Net displacement = 2.5 - 1.25 = 1.25 km
    Total distance travelled = 2.5 + 1.25 = 3.75 km
    Average velocity {tex}= \frac{1.25}{\left(\frac{40}{60}\right)}=\frac{1.25 \times 3}{2}=1.875 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} 
    Average speed {tex}= \frac{3.75}{\left(\frac{40}{60}\right)}=5.625 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Look at the graphs (a) to (d) (figure) carefully and state, with reasons, which of these cannot possibly represent one-dimensional motion of a particle.
question_html:
Look at the graphs (a) to (d) (figure) carefully and state, with reasons, which of these cannot possibly represent one-dimensional motion of a particle.


  4.
solution_text: The given x-t graph, shown in (a), does not represent one-dimensional motion of the particle. This is because a particle cannot have two positions at the same instant of time. The given v-t graph, shown in (b), does not represent one-dimensional motion of the particle. This is because a particle can never have two values of velocity at the same instant of time. The given v-t graph, shown in (c), does not represent one-dimensional motion of the particle. This is because speed being a scalar quantity cannot be negative. The given v-t graph, shown in (d), does not represent one-dimensional motion of the particle. This is because the total path length travelled by the particle cannot decrease with time.
solution_html:
1. The given x-t graph, shown in (a), does not represent one-dimensional motion of the particle. This is because a particle cannot have two positions at the same instant of time.
  2. The given v-t graph, shown in (b), does not represent one-dimensional motion of the particle. This is because a particle can never have two values of velocity at the same instant of time.
  3. The given v-t graph, shown in (c), does not represent one-dimensional motion of the particle. This is because speed being a scalar quantity cannot be negative.
  4. The given v-t graph, shown in (d), does not represent one-dimensional motion of the particle. This is because the total path length travelled by the particle cannot decrease with time.

subject: Physics
question_text: In given figure shows the x-t plot of one-dimensional motion of a particle. Is it correct to say from the graph that the particle moves in a straight line for t < 0 and on a parabolic path for t > 0? If not, suggest a suitable physical context for this graph.
question_html: In given figure shows the x-t plot of one-dimensional motion of a particle. Is it correct to say from the graph that the particle moves in a straight line for t < 0 and on a parabolic path for t > 0? If not, suggest a suitable physical context for this graph.
solution_text: No, because the x-t graph does not represent the trajectory of the path followed by a particle. From the graph, it is noted that at t = 0, x = 0. The above graph can represent the motion of a body falling freely from a tower under gravity.
solution_html: No, because the x-t graph does not represent the trajectory of the path followed by a particle. From the graph, it is noted that at t = 0, x = 0. The above graph can represent the motion of a body falling freely from a tower under gravity.

subject: Physics
question_text: A police van moving on a highway with a speed of 30 km h -1 fires a bullet at a thief's car speeding away in the same direction with a speed of 192 km h -1 . If the muzzle speed of the bullet is 150m s -1 , with what speed does the bullet hit the thief's car? (Note: Obtain that speed which is relevant for damaging the thief’s car).
question_html:
A police van moving on a highway with a speed of 30 km h-1 fires a bullet at a thief's car speeding away in the same direction with a speed of 192 km h-1. If the muzzle speed of the bullet is 150m s-1, with what speed does the bullet hit the thief's car?
(Note: Obtain that speed which is relevant for damaging the thief’s car).
solution_text: Speed of the police van, v p = 30 km/h = 8.33 m/s Muzzle speed of the bullet, v b = 150 m/s Speed of the thief's car, v t = 192 km/h = 53.33 m/s Since the bullet is fired from a moving van, its resultant speed can be obtained as: = 150 + 8.33 = 158.33 m/s Since both the vehicles are moving in the same direction, the velocity with which the bullet hits the thief's car can be obtained as: v bt = v b - v t = 158.33 - 53.33 = 105 m/s
solution_html:
Speed of the police van, vp = 30 km/h = 8.33 m/s
Muzzle speed of the bullet, vb = 150 m/s
Speed of the thief's car, vt = 192 km/h = 53.33 m/s
Since the bullet is fired from a moving van, its resultant speed can be obtained as:
= 150 + 8.33 = 158.33 m/s
Since both the vehicles are moving in the same direction, the velocity with which the bullet hits the thief's car can be obtained as:
vbt = vb - vt = 158.33 - 53.33 = 105 m/s

subject: Physics
question_text: Suggest a suitable physical situation for each of the following graphs:
question_html: Suggest a suitable physical situation for each of the following graphs:
solution_text: The given x-t graph shows that initially a body was at rest. Then, its velocity increases with time and attains an instantaneous constant value. The velocity then reduces to zero with an increase in time. Then, its velocity increases with time in the opposite direction and acquires a constant value. A similar physical situation arises when a football (initially kept at rest) is kicked and gets rebound from a rigid wall so that its speed gets reduced. Then, it passes from the player who has kicked it and ultimately gets stopped after sometime. In the given v-t graph, the sign of velocity changes and its magnitude decreases with a passage of time. A similar situation arises when a ball is dropped on the hard floor from a height. It strikes the floor with some velocity and upon rebound, its velocity decreases by a factor. This continues till the velocity of the ball eventually becomes zero. The given a-t graph reveals that initially the body is moving with a certain uniform velocity. Its acceleration increases for a short interval of time, which again drops to zero. This indicates that the body again starts moving with the same constant velocity. A similar physical situation arises when a hammer moving with a uniform velocity strikes a nail.
solution_html:
1. The given x-t graph shows that initially a body was at rest. Then, its velocity increases with time and attains an instantaneous constant value. The velocity then reduces to zero with an increase in time. Then, its velocity increases with time in the opposite direction and acquires a constant value. A similar physical situation arises when a football (initially kept at rest) is kicked and gets rebound from a rigid wall so that its speed gets reduced. Then, it passes from the player who has kicked it and ultimately gets stopped after sometime.
  2. In the given v-t graph, the sign of velocity changes and its magnitude decreases with a passage of time. A similar situation arises when a ball is dropped on the hard floor from a height. It strikes the floor with some velocity and upon rebound, its velocity decreases by a factor. This continues till the velocity of the ball eventually becomes zero.
  3. The given a-t graph reveals that initially the body is moving with a certain uniform velocity. Its acceleration increases for a short interval of time, which again drops to zero. This indicates that the body again starts moving with the same constant velocity. A similar physical situation arises when a hammer moving with a uniform velocity strikes a nail.

subject: Physics
question_text: A man walks on a straight road from his home to a market 2.5 km away with a speed of 5 kmh -1 . Finding the market closed, he instantly turns and walks back home with a speed of 7.5 km/h. What is the magnitude of average velocity, and the average speed of the man over the interval of time 0 to 30 min, 0 to 50 min, 0 to 40 min? We have carefully distinguished between average speed and magnitude of average velocity. No such distinction is necessary when we consider instantaneous speed and magnitude of velocity. The instantaneous speed is always equal to the magnitude of instantaneous velocity. Why?
question_html:
A man walks on a straight road from his home to a market 2.5 km away with a speed of 5 kmh-1. Finding the market closed, he instantly turns and walks back home with a speed of 7.5 km/h. What is the

  1. magnitude of average velocity, and
  2. the average speed of the man over the interval of time
    1. 0 to 30 min,
    2. 0 to 50 min,
    3. 0 to 40 min? 

We have carefully distinguished between average speed and magnitude of average velocity. No such distinction is necessary when we consider instantaneous speed and magnitude of velocity. The instantaneous speed is always equal to the magnitude of instantaneous velocity. Why?
solution_text: Time taken by the man to reach the market from home, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{1}=\frac{2.5}{5}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{h}=30 \mathrm{min}{/tex} Time taken by the man to reach home from the market, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2}=\frac{2.5}{7.5}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{h}=20 \mathrm{min}{/tex} Total time taken in the whole journey = 30 + 20 = 50 min 0 to 30 min Average speed {tex}= \frac{\text { Distance }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{2.5}{\frac{1}{2}}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} Average velocity {tex}= \frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{2.5}{\frac{1}{2}}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} 0 to 50 min Time = 50 min {tex}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{h}{/tex} Net displacement = 0 Total distance = 2.5 + 2.5 = 5 km Average Velocity {tex}= \frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}=0{/tex} Average Speed {tex}= \frac{\text { Distan ce }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{5}{\left(\frac{5}{6}\right)}=6 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h} \quad {/tex} 0 to 40 min speed of the man = {tex}7.5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h} \quad {/tex} Distance travelled in first 30 min = 2.5 km Distance travelled by the man (from market to home) in the next 10 minutes {tex}= 7.5 \times \frac{10}{60}=1.25 \mathrm{km}{/tex} Net displacement = 2.5 - 1.25 = 1.25 km Total distance travelled = 2.5 + 1.25 = 3.75 km Average velocity {tex}= \frac{1.25}{\left(\frac{40}{60}\right)}=\frac{1.25 \times 3}{2}=1.875 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} Average speed {tex}= \frac{3.75}{\left(\frac{40}{60}\right)}=5.625 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} Instantaneous velocity is defined as the first derivative of distance with respect to time, v in = {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} m/s The time interval dt is very small such that the direction particle doesn’t change. Since velocity and speed differ in direction only. Thus, Instantaneous velocity is equal to the Instantaneous speed always.
solution_html:
Time taken by the man to reach the market from home, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{1}=\frac{2.5}{5}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{h}=30 \mathrm{min}{/tex}
Time taken by the man to reach home from the market, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2}=\frac{2.5}{7.5}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{h}=20 \mathrm{min}{/tex}
Total time taken in the whole journey = 30 + 20 = 50 min

  1. 0 to 30 min
    Average speed {tex}= \frac{\text { Distance }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{2.5}{\frac{1}{2}}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex}
    Average velocity {tex}= \frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{2.5}{\frac{1}{2}}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex}
  2. 0 to 50 min Time = 50 min {tex}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{h}{/tex}
    Net displacement = 0
    Total distance = 2.5 + 2.5 = 5 km
    Average Velocity {tex}= \frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}=0{/tex} 
    Average Speed {tex}= \frac{\text { Distan ce }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{5}{\left(\frac{5}{6}\right)}=6 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h} \quad {/tex}
  3. 0 to 40 min
    speed of the man = {tex}7.5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h} \quad {/tex}
    Distance travelled in first 30 min = 2.5 km
    Distance travelled by the man (from market to home) in the next 10 minutes
    {tex}= 7.5 \times \frac{10}{60}=1.25 \mathrm{km}{/tex}
    Net displacement = 2.5 - 1.25 = 1.25 km
    Total distance travelled = 2.5 + 1.25 = 3.75 km
    Average velocity {tex}= \frac{1.25}{\left(\frac{40}{60}\right)}=\frac{1.25 \times 3}{2}=1.875 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex} 
    Average speed {tex}= \frac{3.75}{\left(\frac{40}{60}\right)}=5.625 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex}

Instantaneous velocity is defined as the first derivative of distance with respect to time, vin = {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} m/s
The time interval dt is very small such that the direction particle doesn’t change. Since velocity and speed differ in direction only.
Thus, Instantaneous velocity is equal to the Instantaneous speed always.

subject: Physics
question_text: Figure gives the x-t plot of a particle executing one-dimensional simple harmonic motion. Give the signs of position, velocity and acceleration variables of the particle at t = 0.3 s, 1.2 s, -1.2 s.
question_html: Figure gives the x-t plot of a particle executing one-dimensional simple harmonic motion. Give the signs of position, velocity and acceleration variables of the particle at t = 0.3 s, 1.2 s, -1.2 s.
solution_text: For simple harmonic motion (SHM) of a particle, acceleration (a) is given by the relation: a = {tex}- \omega ^ { 2 } x{/tex} …..(i) where {tex}\omega{/tex} angular frequency and {tex}x ={/tex} displacement and velocity of the particle, v = {tex}\frac {dx}{dt}{/tex} .....(ii) where {tex}\frac {dx}{dt} ={/tex} slope of x-t plot Now at t = 0.3 s In this time interval, x is negative. Thus, the slope of the x-t plot will also be negative. From equation (ii) again, velocity is the slope of x-t plot. Therefore, both position and velocity are negative. However, using equation (i), acceleration of the particle will be positive. Now at t = 1.2 s In this time interval, x is positive. Thus, the slope of the x-t plot i.e. the velocity of the particle will also be positive from equation (ii). Therefore, both position and velocity are positive. However, using equation (i), acceleration of the particle comes to be negative. And at t = -1.2 s In this time interval, x is negative and t is also negative. Hence, the slope of the x-t plot i.e. the velocity of the particle will be positive here from equation (ii). From equation (i), it can be inferred that the acceleration of the particle will be positive, as x is negative.
solution_html:
For simple harmonic motion (SHM) of a particle, acceleration (a) is given by the relation:
a = {tex}- \omega ^ { 2 } x{/tex}…..(i)
where {tex}\omega{/tex} angular frequency and {tex}x ={/tex} displacement
and velocity of the particle, v = {tex}\frac {dx}{dt}{/tex} .....(ii)
where {tex}\frac {dx}{dt} ={/tex} slope of x-t plot
Now at t = 0.3 s
In this time interval, x is negative. Thus, the slope of the x-t plot will also be negative. From equation (ii) again, velocity is the slope of x-t plot. Therefore, both position and velocity are negative. However, using equation (i), acceleration of the particle will be positive.
Now at t = 1.2 s
In this time interval, x is positive. Thus, the slope of the x-t plot i.e. the velocity of the particle will also be positive from equation (ii).
Therefore, both position and velocity are positive. However, using equation (i), acceleration of the particle comes to be negative.
And at t = -1.2 s
In this time interval, x is negative and t is also negative. Hence, the slope of the x-t plot i.e. the velocity of the particle will be positive here from equation (ii). From equation (i), it can be inferred that the acceleration of the particle will be positive, as x is negative.

subject: Physics
question_text: Figure gives the x-t plot of a particle in one-dimensional motion. Three different equal intervals of time are shown. In which interval is the average speed greatest, and in which is it the least? Give the sign of average velocity for each interval.
question_html: Figure gives the x-t plot of a particle in one-dimensional motion. Three different equal intervals of time are shown. In which interval is the average speed greatest, and in which is it the least? Give the sign of average velocity for each interval.
solution_text: The average speed of a particle shown in the x-t graph is obtained from the slope of the graph in a particular interval of time. It is clear from the graph that the slope is maximum and minimum respectively in intervals 3 and 2 respectively. Therefore, the average speed of the particle is the greatest in interval 3 and is the least in interval 2. The sign of average velocity is positive in both intervals 1 and 2 as the slope is positive in these intervals. However, it is negative in interval 3 because the slope is negative in this interval.
solution_html: The average speed of a particle shown in the x-t graph is obtained from the slope of the graph in a particular interval of time. It is clear from the graph that the slope is maximum and minimum respectively in intervals 3 and 2 respectively. Therefore, the average speed of the particle is the greatest in interval 3 and is the least in interval 2. The sign of average velocity is positive in both intervals 1 and 2 as the slope is positive in these intervals. However, it is negative in interval 3 because the slope is negative in this interval.

subject: Physics
question_text: Figure gives a speed-time graph of a particle in motion along a constant direction. Three equal intervals of time are shown. In which interval is the average acceleration greatest in magnitude? In which interval is the average speed greatest? Choosing the positive direction as the constant direction of motion, give the signs of v and {tex}a{/tex} in the three intervals. What are the accelerations at the points A, B, C and D?
question_html: Figure gives a speed-time graph of a particle in motion along a constant direction. Three equal intervals of time are shown. In which interval is the average acceleration greatest in magnitude? In which interval is the average speed greatest? Choosing the positive direction as the constant direction of motion, give the signs of v and {tex}a{/tex} in the three intervals. What are the accelerations at the points A, B, C and D?
solution_text: As the change of speed is greatest in interval 2, So magnitude of the acceleration is greatest in interval 2. Height of the curve from the time-axis gives the average speed of the particle. It is apparent from the graph that height is the greatest in interval 3. Hence, the average speed of the particle is the greatest in interval 3. Velocity is positive in all the intervals. Acceleration is positive in interval 1 and 3 as speed is increasing while it is negative in interval 2 as speed is decreasing. Points A, B, C, and D are all parallel to the time-axis. Hence, the slope is zero at these points. Therefore, at points A, B, C, and D, acceleration of the particle is zero.
solution_html:
1. As the change of speed is greatest in interval 2, So magnitude of the acceleration is greatest in interval 2.
  2. Height of the curve from the time-axis gives the average speed of the particle. It is apparent from the graph that height is the greatest in interval 3. Hence, the average speed of the particle is the greatest in interval 3.
  3. Velocity is positive in all the intervals. Acceleration is positive in interval 1 and 3 as speed is increasing while it is negative in interval 2 as speed is decreasing.
  4. Points A, B, C, and D are all parallel to the time-axis. Hence, the slope is zero at these points. Therefore, at points A, B, C, and D, acceleration of the particle is zero.
